title: Pull-Out Sorting Laundry Station
summary: A tabletop that conceals a rolling three-bin sorting cart underneath — the cart rides on drawer slides and casters, pulling out from under the table like a giant drawer.
description:
This laundry station is really two separate builds that combine into one: a simple 2x4-leg table, and a rolling cart sized to slide underneath it on drawer slides like an oversized drawer. Build the table frame first exactly like any simple leg table, with pocket holes throughout and the top attached last — this piece is genuinely simple and stands complete on its own before the cart is ever built.

The cart is where the real trick lives: **it's built as a simple 1x3 frame box on caster wheels, sized to hold three canvas sorting bins**, with its own drawer slide member installed to mate with the one under the tabletop. Build the cart's face frames first, then whatever bottom material you have on hand — the plan is genuinely flexible here, listing scrap plywood, various 1x boards, or a full plywood panel as equally valid options.

Once both pieces are built, position the cart inside the table frame and install the drawer slides connecting them — **this is the step that turns two separate pieces of furniture into one functioning unit**, letting the whole sorting cart roll out from under the table on its casters for loading and unloading laundry, then push back in flush when not in use.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood or project panel, 18 in x 48 in
Canvas laundry bins, 14 in x 14 in
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.material: 3/4" plywood or project panel, 18 in x 48 in

cutList.note: Or use scrap plywood cut to fit.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: From the 12 ft 2x4, cut four table legs 35 1/4" — 141" of a 144" board, so square one end first and work straight down the stick. From the six 8 ft 1x3s, cut four table side supports 11", three table front and back supports 45", four cart legs 21 1/2", four cart front and back pieces 39", four cart sides 14" and four cart bottom pieces 14". That fills the six boards with very little to spare, so measure twice. The 3/4" panel is the 18" x 48" tabletop, used as supplied. Note how the legs have to be oriented: 45" between them plus 1 1/2" of leg at each end makes 48", and 11" plus 3 1/2" at each end makes 18" — exactly the tabletop, which is how you know the 3 1/2" faces belong on the ends.
materials: 3/4" plywood or project panel, 18 in x 48 in

steps.title: Build the table legs
steps.body: Drill two 3/4" pocket holes in each end of the four 11" side supports, and drill a row of holes facing upward along the top edge of the two that will sit at the top — those upward holes are how the tabletop attaches later and they are very hard to add once the frame is together. Join two legs and two side supports into a flat end assembly, one support at the top and one lower down, checking with the square that the legs stay parallel and the supports stay perpendicular. Fasten with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws. Build both end assemblies on the same flat surface and stand them side by side to confirm they match.

steps.title: Complete the table frame
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes in both ends of the three 45" front and back supports, plus upward-facing holes along the top edges of the two that will sit at the top. Join the two end assemblies with one 45" support across the top front and one across the top back, and put the third lower as a stretcher. Fasten with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue, then measure both diagonals across the top opening and correct until they match — the cart has to slide into this opening squarely, and a racked frame will bind the slides.

steps.title: Attach the table top
steps.body: Set the 18" x 48" panel on the frame, flush all round, and fasten it with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws driven up through the holes you already drilled in the top edges of the supports. Work from the center outward so the panel cannot creep as you go. The table is now complete and stands on its own; set it aside on a flat floor and build the cart to fit it.
materials: 3/4" plywood or project panel, 18 in x 48 in

steps.title: Build the cart face frames
steps.body: Each cart face frame is two 21 1/2" legs joined by two 39" pieces, one at the top and one at the bottom — build two of them, identical. Drill 3/4" pocket holes in the ends of the 39" pieces, join with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue, and check the two frames against each other before the glue sets. Then stand them parallel and tie them together with the four 14" side pieces, two at each end, which sets the cart's width. Roll the finished box up to the table's opening before you go further: it has to clear the legs on both sides with room left over for the drawer slides.

steps.title: Add the cart bottom
steps.body: Lay the four 14" bottom pieces across the bottom rails, glue them and nail or screw them down. Four pieces of 1x3 at 2 1/2" wide cover only about 10" of the cart's 39" length, so they will not floor the whole thing — either space them directly under where the bins will sit, or do what the plan's own note suggests and cut a piece of scrap plywood to fill the whole opening. The plywood is the better choice here: three loaded laundry bins are heavier than they look, and a continuous floor also stiffens the cart.

steps.title: Attach caster wheels
steps.body: Attach the four casters to the cart's bottom rails, one near each corner, screwing into the 1x3 frame rather than into the bottom boards. Keep the screws short enough that they cannot come through the top face of the rail where a bin will sit. With the casters on, roll the cart into the table opening and check the height: the top of the cart has to clear the underside of the table's supports, and this is the last easy moment to adjust it.

steps.title: Install the drawer slides
steps.body: Position the cart inside the table frame at the height it will run at, blocked up on scrap so it stays put, then mount the 16" full-extension slides — cabinet member to the table's side supports, drawer member to the cart's side rails, both dead level and at the same height on each side. Slides even slightly out of parallel will bind as soon as the cart is loaded. Pull the cart out and push it back several times empty before you trust it with bins in it, and adjust while the screws are still in their slots.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand every surface, easing the tabletop's edges where laundry gets folded, then apply primer and paint, or stain and poly, finishing the cart and the table separately so you can reach inside both. Drop the three 14" x 14" canvas bins in — note that three 14" bins want 42" of run while the cart's front and back rails are 39", so expect them to sit snug or plan on two plus a smaller one. Fit the long handle across the cart's front so there is something to pull on.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly
Canvas laundry bins, 14 in x 14 in

images.alt: Laundry sorting cart that rolls out from under a tabletop
